skills.name: cerberus:leak-check
skills.activates-when: Use before committing a NEW or EDITED skill, its references, or its eval fixtures to this PUBLIC marketplace — review the change for anything that points to a real work codebase (real class/object/namespace/org/ticket names, secrets, real people/emails/paths, an employer or client brand, or the aggregate "this is one specific company's product" flavor) and rewrite it to a neutral fictional demo before it ships. Also runs when the CERBERUS hook flags a skill/eval edit.
skills.purpose: Agent judgment pass that reviews a skill/reference/eval change for anything pointing to a real work codebase — secrets, real people/emails/paths, employer or client brand, real namespace/object names, or the aggregate "one company's product" flavor — and rewrites it to a neutral fictional demo.
skills.best-for: Any contributor to this public skills marketplace, right before committing a new or edited skill, reference, or eval fixture — also triggered automatically by the cerberus PostToolUse hook on skill edits.

skills.name: diagram:diagram
skills.activates-when: Use when creating or refining an architecture / flow diagram (a feature "schema", data flow, or how-it-works picture) that should end up readable and user-friendly — renders a D2 source with the ELK layout engine and inlines it into a self-contained, committable HTML page. Also use when iterating on an existing diagram to improve readability.
skills.purpose: Turn a spec/code scope into a hardened entity Atlas, then a readable D2→ELK architecture diagram inlined into a self-contained, committable HTML page.
skills.best-for: Teams who want an accurate, clickable "how it works" diagram (classes/objects/permission sets + relations) committed alongside a spec or module instead of a hand-drawn or memory-derived picture.

changes.notes: Writes a new docs/diagrams/<name>/ folder — atlas.md, diagram.html, and assets/ (graph.d2, page.html, copied CSS/JS/svg-pan-zoom, rendered SVG/PNG) — via the bundled references/build.sh script. Does not touch git, network, org, or money.

skills.name: frontend:fe-check
skills.activates-when: Typecheck and run (targeted) unit tests for a frontend repo, returning a terse one-block summary. Use after editing TypeScript/React to quickly confirm types are clean and the relevant tests pass — instead of running tsc and vitest separately and reading full output.
skills.purpose: Typecheck a frontend repo with tsc and run (optionally targeted) vitest tests, printing one terse pass/fail summary instead of two noisy tool outputs.
skills.best-for: Any TypeScript/React repo with tsc + vitest configured, pulled in right after editing code to confirm types and relevant tests still pass before moving on.

changes.notes: Runs `tsc --noEmit` and `vitest run` via the bundled fe-check.sh script — read-only checks that touch no source files, git state, network, or org; may write local tsc/vitest cache/output files as a side effect of the tools it invokes.

skills.name: frontend-css:rem
skills.activates-when: CSS Units \u2014 enforce rem for CSS/SCSS/Tailwind dimensions. Use whenever a hardcoded pixel length appears in a stylesheet or component's layout \u2014 spacing, sizing, or typography such as gap, padding, margin, width, height, or font-size \u2014 and the user wants it changed, reviewed, made consistent with a spacing scale, or made responsive to browser zoom/font-size. Applies whether the value is stated as px, an arbitrary Tailwind value like p-[10px], or an SCSS property. Do NOT trigger for genuinely pixel-appropriate values that stay in px: border-width, box-shadow offsets, or SVG attributes like width, height, and stroke-width on chart/vector elements.
skills.purpose: Enforce rem (not px) for CSS/SCSS/Tailwind dimensional values so layouts respect user font-size/zoom preferences.
skills.best-for: Frontend projects with a shared CSS/SCSS/Tailwind codebase that want consistent, accessible, zoom-responsive sizing instead of scattered hardcoded px.

changes.notes: Advisory convention skill — guides how CSS dimensions should be written/reviewed; it does not itself run any command or modify files.

skills.name: frontend-css:scss-modules
skills.activates-when: House rules for authoring component SCSS — the .scss-over-.css convention, SCSS file structure, the color/spacing/radius variable & token system (never hardcode hex or magic numbers — add to variables first), and BEM structure (block → &__element → &--modifier). Use when writing or refactoring a component's styles, setting up the SCSS token/variable system, or consolidating hardcoded values to the scale. NOT for choosing a dimensional unit or converting px→rem (frontend-css:rem), naming a component's folder or its non-style files (frontend-react:component-structure), style import-alias policy (frontend-js:conventions), LWC/Aura styling (salesforce:lwc_development), or skeleton/Storybook files.
skills.purpose: House rules for authoring component SCSS — SCSS-over-CSS, the color/spacing/radius token system, BEM structure, and when position:absolute is/isn't justified.
skills.best-for: Frontend projects with their own bundler (Vite/Webpack/Next.js, non-Salesforce) that want a consistent SCSS token system and BEM convention instead of scattered hardcoded values.

changes.notes: Pure advisory/convention skill — gives written guidance on SCSS structure and review checklist items; it does not itself edit files or run commands.

skills.name: frontend-js:conventions
skills.activates-when: House JavaScript/TypeScript coding style — arrow functions, single quotes, full variable names (not sel/fn/e), braces on all control structures, small readable functions, and imports (a registered path alias always beats a ../../../ relative import; register one @alias per top-level dir in vite + tsconfig). Use whenever writing, editing, or reviewing JS/TS (React, Node, any JS/TS) — including renaming a variable to a full name, breaking up a long function, converting quotes or adding braces, replacing a deep relative import with an alias, registering a new @alias, or a pre-push style review. NOT for SCSS/styling (frontend-css:scss-modules), scaffolding a component's folder (frontend-react:component-structure), i18n strings, or Apex.
skills.purpose: House JS/TS coding style — arrow functions, single quotes, full variable names, braces on all control structures, small readable functions, and alias-over-relative-import rules.
skills.best-for: Any project writing or reviewing JS/TS (React, Node, or plain JS/TS) that wants consistent house style enforced on every edit.

skills.name: frontend-react:component-placement
skills.activates-when: Entry point BEFORE creating ANY React component, in any React codebase — search for an existing one first, decide placement (primitive / feature / layout / page-local), then route to the matching per-type skill. Framework-agnostic: governs component ARCHITECTURE, not any specific UI or styling library. Activate whenever about to add, scaffold, or hand-roll a component, panel, card, widget, or control.
skills.purpose: Entry-point decision guide that makes you search for an existing React component before building a new one, then routes placement (primitive/feature/layout/page-local) to the matching per-type skill.
skills.best-for: Any team building React UI that wants to stop duplicate components and enforce a consistent placement convention before scaffolding.

skills.name: frontend-react:component-structure
skills.activates-when: Use when creating, editing, restructuring, or reviewing a React frontend component \u2014 including its folder layout and file organization (tsx/scss/index), where a component or sub-component should live, BEM class naming, SCSS styling, rem sizing, i18n of user-visible strings, barrel exports, and replacing inline JS style/hover handlers (e.g. onMouseEnter toggling classes) with CSS. Covers \"this component file is in the wrong place \u2014 fix the structure\", moving a flat .tsx into its own folder, and any question about how a component's files, styles, or markup should be organized.
skills.purpose: Standardizes how a React component's files, folder, BEM classes, and styling conventions are organized so structure stays consistent across the codebase.
skills.best-for: Teams building a React app who want every component (and sub-component) to follow the same folder/file/BEM/i18n/error-boundary conventions instead of drifting per-author.

changes.notes: Pure advisory/convention skill — it guides how a human or agent should structure component files, styles, and markup, but performs no actions itself.

skills.name: frontend-react:feature-components
skills.activates-when: Rules for FEATURE components in a React codebase — domain-coupled blocks that compose primitives and hold business logic/data. Activate when building or editing a domain feature (a panel/table/editor tied to your business data), or adding a new domain area. Framework-agnostic. Reached via frontend-react:component-placement.
skills.purpose: Rules for structuring domain-coupled "feature" components — composed from primitives, business logic kept out of primitives, data via the project's data layer.
skills.best-for: Teams building a React app's domain/feature panels who want features assembled from primitives instead of hand-rolled controls duplicating the primitive library.

skills.name: frontend-react:hooks-registry
skills.activates-when: Use when about to add, hand-roll, rename, move, or remove a custom React `use*` hook.
skills.purpose: Keep custom React hooks deduplicated and discoverable by requiring reuse-first checks and a maintained docs/hooks-registry.md.
skills.best-for: Teams with a growing React codebase where duplicate/overlapping use* hooks keep creeping in and need a single source of truth.

changes.notes: Pure advisory/convention skill — it directs the agent to read and hand-edit docs/hooks-registry.md; the skill itself runs no script and touches nothing on its own.

skills.name: frontend-react:layout-components
skills.activates-when: Rules for LAYOUT / app-chrome components in a React codebase — the shell, top bar, sidebar, command palette, and global overlays that frame every page. Activate when editing or adding app chrome. Framework-agnostic. Reached via frontend-react:component-placement.
skills.purpose: Rules for building app-chrome layout components (shell, top bar, sidebar, command palette, global overlays) that stay free of domain logic.
skills.best-for: Teams building a React app shell/nav who want layout kept separate from feature/domain components and routed through project primitives and config.

skills.name: frontend-react:skeleton-components
skills.activates-when: When building or editing a React component that renders asynchronously-loaded data, decide whether it needs a loading skeleton — and if so, build a separate skeleton component colocated next to it from the shared Skeleton primitive. Activate whenever a component gains a loading state, shows a blank/"Loading…" gap, or you are reviewing async data UI.
skills.purpose: Decide whether an async-loading React component actually needs a loading skeleton, and if so, house rules for building it as a colocated sibling composed from the shared Skeleton primitive.
skills.best-for: React codebases with async data-fetching components (TanStack Query, await, isLoading) that want a consistent, non-duplicated approach to loading states instead of ad hoc blank screens or hand-rolled pulse markup.

skills.name: frontend-react:storybook-stories
skills.activates-when: Use this skill whenever writing, adding, or asking about a Storybook story for a React/UI component \u2014 including \"add a story for X\", \"write a .stories file\", questions about which states/variants a story should cover (default, empty, error, loading, disabled), how to show a component's skeleton alongside it in a story, what mock data a story should use, or whether a just-finished component needs a story at all. Also use right after creating any new React component to decide whether a story is warranted: on the first component in a project, explain Storybook and ask the user yes/no, record the choice in CLAUDE.md, then honor it for every component after. Do NOT use for general code review, component file placement, hooks, i18n, or repo/CI setup scripts.
skills.purpose: Writes a colocated Storybook story (with a separate mock-data file) covering a component's key states, after asking once per project whether stories are wanted at all.
skills.best-for: Projects using Storybook where the team wants every new/finished React component to get a reviewable, state-driving story without re-litigating the yes/no decision each time.

changes.notes: Creates/edits a `<Component>.stories.tsx` and a `<Component>.mock.ts` under the component's `stories/` folder, and adds or updates one `Storybook stories: yes/no` line in the project's CLAUDE.md to record the per-project preference.

skills.name: frontend-react:ui-primitive-reuse
skills.activates-when: Before creating ANY shared/reusable UI primitive in a React codebase (button, input, checkbox, radio, switch, select, textarea, dialog, tooltip, dropdown, badge, card, separator…), search the project's primitive library first to avoid duplicates — reuse or extend what exists; build new only when truly absent. Framework-agnostic (no specific UI or styling library assumed). Activate whenever about to add or hand-roll a reusable field/control/primitive.
skills.purpose: Before hand-rolling a shared UI primitive (button, input, dialog, tooltip, etc.), search the project's existing primitive library and reuse or extend it instead of creating a duplicate.
skills.best-for: React codebases with (or growing) a shared primitives folder, pulled in whenever someone is about to add a new reusable field/control/overlay component.

changes.notes: Advisory/search-first discipline — it searches and reads the existing codebase and defers actual file creation to other placement/structure skills; it does not itself create, edit, or run anything.

skills.name: git:commit
skills.activates-when: Use this skill whenever the user asks to commit, create a commit, or push changes — e.g. "commit this", "let's commit", "make a commit". First syncs docs/skills owned by the changed area, then splits into atomic logical commits.
skills.purpose: Split staged/unstaged changes into atomic, well-ordered conventional-commit-style commits, after first syncing any docs/skills owned by the changed area.
skills.best-for: Personal or small-team repos where the user wants commits auto-split by logical concern instead of committing everything in one lump.

changes.notes: Runs git status/diff read-only first, then actually mutates repo state — git restore --staged ., git add <files>, and git commit — to create the split commits; may also edit docs/skill files as part of the pre-commit sync step.

skills.name: i18n:ui-strings
skills.activates-when: Route every user-facing UI string through the project's localization system instead of hardcoding it. Activate whenever writing or editing display text — labels, buttons, messages, errors, toasts, placeholders, empty states, flavor copy — in any project and any stack.
skills.purpose: Route every user-facing UI string through the project's localization system (or Custom Labels on Salesforce) instead of hardcoding it.
skills.best-for: Any project with user-facing UI text — teams that want new labels/messages/toasts to land in the existing i18n catalog/namespace rather than as scattered string literals.

changes.notes: Advisory routing skill — tells Claude where a new UI string belongs (i18n catalog, Custom Labels, or a proposed constants module) and how to reference it; it does not itself run any command, deploy, or touch git. Any file edits happen via Claude's normal edit tools, not the skill's own action.

skills.name: jira:comment-style
skills.activates-when: Use whenever writing or posting a comment on a Jira issue/ticket — a status update, decision note, handoff, or "what's done / what's left" note, including via the Atlassian MCP addCommentToJiraIssue tool. Keeps comments short, essence-first, and understandable on the first read.
skills.purpose: Keeps Jira ticket comments short, essence-first, and readable on the first pass instead of rambling status narration.
skills.best-for: Teams that post frequent Jira status/handoff comments and want a consistent, scannable house style.

skills.name: meta:error-handling
skills.activates-when: Use when adding or handling any error path on any layer — throwing from a service/route/controller, mapping an upstream failure, or reading an error on the client (backend or frontend).
skills.purpose: Enforce one Google AIP-193 style error envelope (code/status/message/details) across backend and frontend so every error is handled the same way regardless of layer.
skills.best-for: Any project defining or touching an error path — a new service/route/controller throw, mapping an upstream failure, or a frontend reading an error — that wants one consistent, i18n-ready error contract instead of ad-hoc shapes per layer.

skills.name: meta:lean-writing
skills.activates-when: Use this skill to WRITE terse technical documents. Trigger when asked to write, draft, summarize, present, or update a spec, design doc, RFC, brainstorming/exploration summary, or a decision log of options considered — e.g. "write the spec for…", "draft a design doc…", "write up the options we considered…", "present the brainstorming results…", "summarize the exploration we did…" — even if the user never says "concise". Produce short plain sentences, bullets over prose, no filler; caveman-simple but technically precise. NOT for normal conversation, code, or commit/jira messages, and NOT for reviewing or auditing an already-written doc for clarity or bloat (that's meta:wittgenstein).
skills.purpose: Write terse, caveman-simple specs, design docs, or brainstorming summaries — lead with the decision, bullets over prose, no filler.
skills.best-for: Anyone drafting a spec/design-doc/RFC/decision-log who wants short plain sentences instead of padded prose.

changes.notes: Pure writing-style guidance — produces prose the author places themselves; the skill itself edits nothing.

skills.name: meta:model-routing
skills.activates-when: Use whenever the plan is to spawn, fan out, or parallelize agents \u2014 ANY multi-agent work, not just when models are mentioned. Triggers: \"spawn/kick off subagents\", \"a bunch of agents in parallel\", fleets that audit/comb/sweep/hunt across files, verify or skeptic stages, reviewer fan-outs, loop-until-done runs, authoring or editing a Workflow script (agent()/parallel()/pipeline()), or writing agent-definition frontmatter. Every spawned agent must get an EXPLICIT model tier assigned BEFORE it launches: reserve expensive head models for a small bounded set of final judges/synthesizers; mid-tier for judgment (review, verify, hunt); cheap tier for mechanical sweeps (grep, list, count, rename). Activate even when the user says nothing about models or cost \u2014 an omitted model silently inherits the pricey session model, and fan-out multiplies that waste by the agent count.
skills.purpose: Forces an explicit, cost-tiered model choice (head/work/sweep) on every spawned agent before any multi-agent fan-out, workflow script, or eval loop launches.
skills.best-for: Anyone about to spawn subagents, write a Workflow script with agent()/parallel()/pipeline(), or run an eval/optimization loop, who wants to avoid silently inheriting the expensive session model across every spawned instance.

changes.notes: Advisory only: gives model-tier rules, a spend-estimate table format, and rationalizations to check against before launching a fan-out. It does not itself spawn agents, call APIs, or write files — enforcement happens in whatever Workflow/agent-spawning code the user or another skill runs afterward.

skills.name: meta:new-skill
skills.activates-when: Use this skill to author a brand-new skill FROM SCRATCH \u2014 deciding its plugin/domain, folder name, file placement, and writing its SKILL.md, frontmatter, and initial metadata.yaml sidecar. Triggers for \"create/add/write a new skill for X\", \"where does the SKILL.md go\", \"what's the naming convention or folder structure for a new skill\", or \"what goes in the description frontmatter\". This is about scaffolding a NOT-YET-EXISTING skill's structure and location, NOT about tuning, evaluating, or fixing an EXISTING skill's trigger behavior (skill-creator), and NOT about refreshing an EXISTING skill's metadata.yaml after its body/tools/scripts/hooks changed \u2014 that is the separate `meta:update-skill` skill. Also NOT for ordinary code, components, or config in a project. If the skill already exists, use `meta:update-skill` or `skill-creator` instead.
skills.purpose: Scaffold a brand-new skill in the plugin model and author its metadata.yaml via a field-by-field interview.
skills.best-for: Any marketplace where a new skill must ship a metadata.yaml sidecar and follow the house folder/naming structure.

changes.notes: Creates the skill's SKILL.md and writes its metadata.yaml sidecar on disk.

skills.name: meta:ockham
skills.activates-when: The Razor. Invoke before creating ANY new entity — file, folder, module, class, function, component, hook, util, type, prop, variant, config key, abstraction layer, doc, or top-level category — in ANY language or stack. Not domain-specific. If you are about to bring a new thing into existence, OCKHAM speaks first.
skills.purpose: Before creating any new file/module/class/function/prop/abstraction, forces a three-gate reuse-first check so entities aren't multiplied needlessly.
skills.best-for: Any project that wants a discipline gate against sprawl — teams prone to premature helpers/wrappers/managers or copy-paste-and-rename duplication.

skills.name: meta:solid
skills.activates-when: SOLID is the design law for all code — SRP/OCP/LSP/ISP/DIP plus DRY, KISS, YAGNI. Activate at DESIGN time and whenever writing, editing, or reviewing code in ANY language or stack — ESPECIALLY when a class, function, hook, route, or method does several things (fetches + formats + writes, or auth + validation + DB in one method) and you're weighing whether to split it; a switch or if-chain keeps growing with new cases; you're deciding how one module should depend on or import another; or designing an interface or extension point. Pairs with meta:ockham (whether an entity should exist at all) — SOLID governs how the entities that DO exist are structured.
skills.purpose: Design-time guidance applying SOLID (SRP/OCP/LSP/ISP/DIP) plus DRY/KISS/YAGNI when writing, editing, or reviewing code structure.
skills.best-for: Any project where a team wants consistent structural judgment calls (when to split a function/class, how modules should depend on each other) enforced during coding and review, not just at architecture-doc time.

skills.name: meta:triage
skills.activates-when: Use when you're about to attack a LARGE batch of items the same expensive way \u2014 a repo-wide sweep for dead code, unused exports, or bugs; a big multi-file diff to review; dozens of call sites to migrate; a fleet of skills, files, or docs to audit or read; every TODO to classify; hundreds of failing tests to work through. Any \"do X across the whole repo / all of them / everything\" request where the set is too big to give each item full attention. Instead of paying deep cost on every item, first run one CHEAP shallow pass over ALL of them to sort into skip vs. worth-it, then spend the expensive deep pass only on the shortlist. Invoke at planning time, before touching the first item. NOT for a single file or function (just do it), NOT for picking a model tier (meta:model-routing), NOT for a plain parallel spawn with no sorting step.
skills.purpose: Run a cheap full-coverage sorting pass over a large batch of items before spending an expensive pass only on the ones that need it.
skills.best-for: Anyone about to sweep, review, migrate, or classify a large set of files/items uniformly and wants to avoid paying deep-pass cost on every single one.

skills.name: meta:update-skill
skills.activates-when: Use this skill to REFRESH the metadata.yaml sidecar of an ALREADY-EXISTING skill after its body, allowed-tools, bundled scripts, or hooks changed. Triggers for \"I edited skill X, refresh its metadata\", \"update the metadata.yaml after changing this skill\", \"this skill's behavior changed — does its metadata still match\", or noticing a scout sync-nudge after editing a SKILL.md/script. This is about RE-RUNNING the non-derivable-fields interview on a skill that already has a metadata.yaml, NOT about scaffolding a brand-new skill from scratch (that is `meta:new-skill`), and NOT about tuning an existing skill's trigger description or running skill-creator evals (that is the `skill-creator` plugin).
skills.purpose: Refresh an existing skill's metadata.yaml after its body, tools, scripts, or hooks changed, by re-interviewing the non-derivable fields.
skills.best-for: Any marketplace using metadata.yaml sidecars, to keep a skill's changes/purpose accurate after it is edited.

changes.notes: Rewrites the edited skill's metadata.yaml sidecar on disk.

skills.name: meta:wittgenstein
skills.activates-when: Clarity gate for an already-written spec, plan, design doc/note, or RFC — a reviewer persona, not a writing guide. Activate to REVIEW or AUDIT such a doc (often under docs/superpowers/specs or docs/superpowers/plans) when checking whether a non-technical manager or reader could follow it; when it feels padded, wordy, or has grown long; when a section is a wall of prose or full of undefined jargon; or when the decision is buried at the end and should lead — then fix it in place. NOT for WRITING or drafting a doc from scratch (that's meta:lean-writing — wittgenstein reviews one that already exists), and NOT for chat, code, comments, commit messages, jira, or READMEs.
skills.purpose: Review an already-written spec/plan/design doc/RFC for clarity — cut filler, front-load the bottom line, and rewrite walls of prose into skimmable bullets a non-technical manager could follow.
skills.best-for: Anyone whose spec/plan/design doc has grown long, padded, or jargon-heavy and needs a laconic pass before a non-technical reader signs off on it.

changes.notes: Reviews and rewrites the target document's own prose in place (via Claude's normal edit tools) for clarity and terseness; it runs no command, touches no git, org, or network, and depends on no other skill.

skills.name: review:setup
skills.activates-when: Install and target the pre-push review framework in a repo. Use when a repo has the review plugin but no .claude/review.config.json, when the user asks to set up the review gate / pre-push review / secret-scan CI, or when adopting the 5-agent /review system in a new project.
skills.purpose: Install and tailor the pre-push review framework (5 reviewer agents, /review, secret-scan + attestation gate) into a target repo.
skills.best-for: A repo that has the review plugin enabled but no .claude/review.config.json yet, and wants the 5-agent pre-push review gate adopted and tuned to its stack.

changes.notes: Walks the adopter through running bootstrap.sh/install.sh (fetched via `gh api` over the network), which vendor scripts/review/*, .husky/pre-push, .github/workflows/review-gate.yml, and a seeded .claude/review.config.json into the target repo, and merge the marketplace + plugin entry into the repo's committed .claude/settings.json. The skill itself then hand-edits .claude/review.config.json (zones/skills/rules/pairedDocs) to tailor it to the project.

skills.name: salesforce:apex_test-authoring
skills.activates-when: Use when writing, fixing, or extending an Apex test — writing a test class for a new or changed Apex class, covering a negative or bulk path, adding a @TestSetup data factory, replacing System.assertEquals with Assert.*, removing a hardcoded record Id, mocking a RestRequest for a REST resource, or updating a test after you changed the class under test. Also whenever you create or edit any Apex `.cls` — every new class must get a matching test class in the same change. Covers data factories, @TestSetup, Assert.* assertions, FLS/user-mode testing, REST resource mocking, and bulk/positive/negative coverage.
skills.purpose: Enforces a house standard for writing Apex test classes — Assert.* only, one-behavior-per-method, data factories, @TestSetup, FLS/user-mode coverage, REST mocking, and a mandatory adversarial/negative suite.
skills.best-for: Salesforce projects that want every new or edited Apex class to ship with a rigorous, consistently-structured test class instead of ad-hoc or happy-path-only tests.

skills.name: salesforce:dx_mcp
skills.activates-when: Interact with a Salesforce org through the salesforce-dx MCP tools instead of the raw `sf` CLI. Use for any org-touching task: running SOQL or Tooling queries against an org, running or resuming Apex tests, deploying/pushing or retrieving/pulling metadata and Apex classes to/from an org (scratch, sandbox, or prod), resuming a long-running deploy or test job by id, and resolving which org alias or username to target. Triggers on requests like \"query the org\", \"push/deploy these classes to the org\", \"run the Apex tests on <org>\", \"resume the deploy job\", or \"which org alias should I use\". If the action reaches out to a live org \u2014 data, tests, metadata, or org/alias selection \u2014 reach for this first.
skills.purpose: Directs org-touching work (SOQL/Tooling queries, Apex test runs, metadata deploy/retrieve, org/alias resolution) through the salesforce-dx MCP tools instead of raw `sf` CLI shell calls.
skills.best-for: Any Salesforce project with the salesforce-dx MCP connected, where an agent would otherwise reach for `sf` CLI via Bash to query, deploy, or test against a live org.

changes.notes: The skill itself is advisory (routes to MCP tools, edits nothing directly), but following it drives live org side effects — SOQL/Tooling queries, Apex test runs, and metadata deploy/retrieve against scratch/sandbox/prod orgs via the salesforce-dx MCP tools it names.

skills.name: salesforce:lwc_development
skills.activates-when: House rules for developing Salesforce Lightning Web Components (LWC) and Aura — the .js controller, .html template, .js-meta.xml, and CSS of a bundle, plus every LWC authoring idiom. Use whenever creating or editing an LWC/Aura bundle or writing any Salesforce-side frontend JS — including adding an @api or @track property, wiring an @wire to an Apex class, emitting or handling a custom event, a lifecycle hook, DOM access (querySelector), styling a component (CSS custom properties, scss→css conversion), or writing the js-meta.xml targets / isExposed config.
skills.purpose: House rules for authoring LWC/Aura bundles — DOM access boundaries and CSS-only (no SCSS) styling conventions.
skills.best-for: Any Salesforce project team writing or reviewing LWC/Aura component JS, HTML, or CSS who wants a consistent authoring standard.

skills.name: salesforce:security_review-rules
skills.activates-when: Security rules for reviewing changes or writing security-sensitive code — secret/API-key leakage, BFF route auth, client token boundary, SOQL injection, XSS in Aura/LWC output, WITH USER_MODE / FLS enforcement, and the Salesforce Security Review bar for Apex. Activate when auditing a diff for security (hardcoded secrets, injection, missing auth, token leaks, unescaped output) or writing a BFF route, Apex REST resource, or any auth/SOQL-touching code.
skills.purpose: Paranoid security-review checklist for Salesforce/BFF changes — secret leakage, token boundary, route auth, injection/XSS, and Apex CRUD/FLS/sharing against the AppExchange Security Review bar.
skills.best-for: Teams doing pre-push security review or writing auth/SOQL/BFF-touching code who want a consistent, "guilty until proven safe" checklist instead of ad-hoc eyeballing.

changes.notes: Pure advisory/checklist skill — guides a reviewer's or author's judgment; it does not itself edit files, run commands, touch git, or call any org/network.

skills.name: salesforce:sf-deploy-test
skills.activates-when:
CLI fallback that deploys Salesforce Apex/metadata to an org and returns a compact TWO-LINE pass/fail summary (deploy: Succeeded / tests: X/Y passed) via a bash wrapper over `sf project deploy` + `sf apex run test` — no MCP dependency.

PREFER the salesforce-dx MCP (`mcp__salesforce-dx__deploy_metadata` + `mcp__salesforce-dx__run_apex_test`, per the `dx_mcp` skill) for programmatic deploy/test when the project has it — structured, resumable results, no shell/path friction. Reach for THIS skill instead when: you want a terse one-line pass/fail without parsing MCP JSON; the salesforce-dx MCP is unavailable (headless / cron / MCP server not connected); or you need combined deploy-then-test in a single call. If the project's CLAUDE.md names the dx MCP as the default deploy+test harness, follow that and treat this as the fallback.

Still covers interpreting a failed deploy (source-tracking, unsafe-path, cross-repo shared-object errors). Don't use for anonymous Apex snippets, ad-hoc SOQL, listing orgs, code review, or UI work with no deploy.
skills.purpose: CLI fallback that deploys Apex/metadata to a Salesforce org and runs Apex tests, returning a terse two-line pass/fail summary — no MCP dependency.
skills.best-for: Projects without the salesforce-dx MCP connected (headless/cron use), or anyone who wants a single combined deploy+test call with a one-glance result instead of parsing MCP JSON.

changes.notes: Runs `sf project deploy start` and `sf apex run test` against a live Salesforce org via a bundled shell script — deploys metadata/Apex and executes tests on that org over the network.

skills.name: salesforce:sf-run
skills.activates-when: Run anonymous Apex or a SOQL query against a Salesforce org and get a terse pass/fail result. Use whenever you need to execute anonymous Apex, reproduce an org-side error, or run a quick SOQL check against a dev/scratch org — instead of hand-rolling `sf org display` + curl to the Tooling API.
skills.purpose: Run anonymous Apex or a SOQL query against a Salesforce org via a colocated script, returning a terse pass/fail instead of raw JSON.
skills.best-for: Anyone doing Salesforce org-side dev work who needs a quick anonymous-Apex/SOQL check without hand-rolling the sf CLI token + curl recipe.

changes.notes: Hits the org's Tooling API executeAnonymous/query endpoint over the network; anonymous Apex can run DML against the target org, which is org-mutating by design (per the skill's own warning).

skills.name: scout:scout
skills.activates-when: Use this skill when the user asks what skills exist in this marketplace, what could help with a task, to recommend or install a skill, or to browse the catalog — e.g. \"what skills are available for writing tests?\", \"recommend a skill for reviewing my SCSS\", \"install the skill that formats commit messages\", \"show me everything in this marketplace\". This skill is PASSIVE — it never runs code; it is instructions telling the agent to read the bundled catalog.json and act on it. NOT for authoring a brand-new skill (`meta:new-skill`), NOT for refreshing an existing skill's metadata.yaml (`meta:update-skill`), and NOT for ordinary unrelated coding tasks.
skills.purpose: Guide the agent to discover, recommend, and install marketplace skills from the compiled catalog.
skills.best-for: Any session where the user wants to browse what skills exist, get a fit recommendation for a task, or install a skill from this marketplace.

changes.notes: Read-only — reads the bundled catalog.json and, on explicit user OK, runs /plugin install in-session. Never writes files, never shells out, never touches git.
